attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic for review of her routine morphology ultrasound at 20 weeks gestation. This is her first pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy multivitamins. There is no family history of congenital abnormalities.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her uterine size is consistent with dates. The morphology ultrasound report indicates features suspicious of hypoplastic left heart, including a small left ventricle and ascending aorta.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Urgent referral for fetal echocardiogram and maternal-fetal medicine review
Repeat morphology ultrasound in two weeks
attributes.explanation:
This patient's morphology scan has identified features suggestive of hypoplastic left heart syndrome (HLHS), a severe congenital cardiac defect characterised by underdevelopment of the left heart structures. Given the significant implications for fetal and neonatal outcomes, appropriate specialist assessment is essential.

Urgent referral for fetal echocardiogram and maternal-fetal medicine review is the most appropriate next step. This allows for confirmation of the diagnosis, detailed assessment of cardiac anatomy, and expert counselling regarding prognosis and management options. Early referral is crucial for planning appropriate perinatal care.

Arrange termination of pregnancy counselling would be premature without confirmation of the diagnosis and specialist consultation. While termination may be discussed as part of comprehensive counselling, this should occur after specialist assessment and detailed discussion of all management options.

Repeat morphology ultrasound in two weeks would delay appropriate specialist assessment unnecessarily. When cardiac abnormalities are suspected, immediate referral for fetal echocardiography is indicated rather than repeat routine scanning.

Continue routine antenatal care would be inappropriate given the suspected serious cardiac abnormality. These findings require urgent specialist assessment and cannot be managed in routine antenatal care.

Arrange amniocentesis may be considered as part of the overall assessment, but it is not the most appropriate next step. While chromosomal abnormalities can be associated with cardiac defects, confirming the cardiac diagnosis and obtaining specialist input takes priority.

HLHS requires coordinated multidisciplinary care including maternal-fetal medicine, pediatric cardiology, and neonatal intensive care. If the diagnosis is confirmed, delivery should be planned at a tertiary centre with appropriate cardiac surgical facilities. Parents will need detailed counselling regarding surgical options (including the Norwood procedure) versus palliative care, and the long-term implications for the child.
learningPoints: Suspected fetal cardiac abnormalities on routine morphology ultrasound require urgent referral for fetal echocardiography and maternal-fetal medicine review.
Hypoplastic left heart syndrome is a severe congenital cardiac defect with significant implications for neonatal survival.
Early detection of fetal cardiac abnormalities allows for appropriate counselling, delivery planning, and preparation of neonatal cardiac services.
references: Moon-Grady AJ, Donofrio MT, Gelehrter S, Hornberger L, Kreeger J, Lee W, Michelfelder E, Morris SA, Peyvandi S, Pinto NM, Pruetz J, Sethi N, Simpson J, Srivastava S, Tian Z. Guidelines and Recommendations for Performance of the Fetal Echocardiogram: An Update from the American Society of Echocardiography. J Am Soc Echocardiogr. 2023 Jul;36(7):679-723. doi: 10.1016/j.echo.2023.04.014. Epub 2023 May 24. PMID: 37227365.
Connor JA, Thiagarajan R. Hypoplastic left heart syndrome. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2007 May 11;2:23. doi: 10.1186/1750-1172-2-23. PMID: 17498282; PMCID: PMC1877799.
